speaker: interviewer
text: --four. Tape number three. Interview with Victor Lewis. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Coming back to the Plaszow, already filled out with the people from ghetto. The life was very miserable. The SS were doing whatever they wanted. [BACKGROUND NOISE] They were beating. They were hitting. They were killing. You could see dead people lying on the floor. And they-- all day used to come with the truck, load the truck up, and bring into the Hujowa Gorka. And after, they were lighting a-- a flame. And that's the way how they were finishing people, because they-- we didn't have ovens then. I mean, crematories. They put some gasoline, some wood, and they shot at it by pistol. And flame came out and burned the people up. Beside us, Goeth himself was working between-- in-- in the [? lager ?] in between people, and he was shooting like crazy. Like crazy. Nobody knew who was going to be the next.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just like that then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just like this. Nobody knew who was going to be the next. Just he keep-- his face-- he didn't like this face, he didn't like his eyes, or anything else, he took a gun and he shoot it. Or he'd give the order to somebody else to shoot. Or he took some few people out from one from one row, the other one from the other row, put together a group, and-- and put it up there. We used to call this Hujowa Gorka. And this is very known now. All the books are written about it, that the Gorka mean a hill. And of the hill, of the Hujowa hill, there was a dig out a hole. And whoever was killed was put up there until-- until there was more people. And they blew it up, and burn it up. Sometimes they used to [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] dig graves, and put a mess of people into the grave. And mostly, they were just burning up. Lot of times, they used to bring people from outside who were hidden, were hidden in-- in on the [? IM ?] [? papers, ?] or hidden by Polacks, or hidden in a forest, or hidden some place else. They put together a truck, 20, 30 people, brought them into concentration camp, right on the Hujowa Gorka. They drop it off, kill all of them, and burned again. And those doing this every day. You couldn't even-- if you smelled the-- the air from the-- from the--

speaker: interviewer
text: The burning in the-- [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: --burning, the body is different smell than you burn the wood, or burn something else. Unbelievable, unbelievable. I would say that in-- in Plaszow was killed at least 10,000 or 20,000 people. [BACKGROUND NOISE] Then [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was came a time why I said that my youngest brother came to Plaszow. And he was in Plaszow for a few days. And he was segregated and sent out to another camp. Where, we didn't know then wherever he was. And me and my second brother, Leon, who we kept together. Somehow, we kept holding together by luck. Then came a big transportation, and they took my brother away. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was alone. But I forgot to mention this, that the-- on 13 of March when they liquidated ghetto in Krakow, and they brought everybody up to Plaszow, to the concentration in Plaszow, my wife came with the transport up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, but you weren't married yet at that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were not married. We were dating. We were sweethearts. We were dating. We start to date in 1936, way before-- before the war. [BACKGROUND NOISE] And I saw my wife almost every day. And did not have how it would work to help because it was hard to help with anything. You had to help yourself. You had eat what they give you. You had to dress what they give you. You had to go to-- to shower and shower the time what they wanted. And that's how we lived in up there. Then on-- on [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] August, I think, August, 1944, my wife-- my youngest brother was-- was sent out from the-- from Plaszow concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so he was there approximately a year then.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, he--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was only few days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: I told you he was working for-- for Kabel. And the Kabel was liquidated a few days before--

speaker: interviewer
text: All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --The moved those people out. So we didn't know where he went. But we-- we knew that the Russian front is coming closer. This was 1944-- late-- late 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Russians were being defeated at the-- I mean, they were--

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm talking the Russia front was coming closer, already back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russian from-- the front was coming close. It was already in Lemberg, in Lviv. It-- it was--

speaker: interviewer
text: The Germans were retreating.

speaker: interviewee
text: Germans were retreating. We knew about it, because some people have radios and listen to it in the night. [BACKGROUND NOISE] And-- and the word spread out. So they start to liquidate the concentration camp Plaszow. And my youngest brother went with one group. I couldn't save him. Then my wife went the other group to Auschwitz. I knew that the transport was going to Auschwitz. Then my next brother went to other camps. I didn't know where-- where he's going to go. Then, after the three transports, there was a very small group. The group was-- was maybe 5,000 people, from around 20,000, 30,000. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Also, I want to tell you a story what happened to me in the same time. I told you that people are-- were helping themself in Plaszow concentration camp. I was electrician, and I had the right to work around whatever I wanted. And I met an SS man who was the foreman in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when you breaking stones. How you--

speaker: interviewer
text: Quarry. A quarry.

speaker: interviewee
text: Quarry. And talking to him, I don't know what occasion it was. [BACKGROUND NOISE] He seems to be a nice guy. So I ask him, if you have anything to sell, I would buy from you. So he asked me what do you want to buy? I said cigarettes, or bread-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] --or a piece of butter, or a piece of wurst. Whatever you gonna-- so he said, all right, tomorrow, maybe I'm going to have some. And then tomorrow came tomorrow, and he told me he had some cigarettes to sell me. [BACKGROUND NOISE] So when that cigarette cost, for instance, $1.00, I had to pay him $20.00-- 20 zloties. I'm talking about zloties.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: 20 Zloties. Then I met-- I met some--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you get the zloties from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there-- there was zloties around in--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: --in camp. People had money. People are coming in from freedom into the concentration camp. They brought something with them. They-- they were hiding. People had dollars. People had gold. [INAUDIBLE] were hiding in-- in their shoes or some place. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] What did I-- What did I miss?

speaker: interviewer
text: You were talking about the SS man bringing you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and I made some-- with him some business for pretty long time. until [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] time came that Germans, and the Nazis knew that the Plaszow gonna be liquidated. He came up and said, I have 10 pack of cigarettes. It was all right. I buy from him. I took the cigarettes. I put behind my shirt. And I was walking away from him, like-- like every time what I  make the-- I didn't make this every day, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --once a while. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I start to moving away from him. And he grabbed me by hand. And he said, now, Victor. Victor, you lived a long, and this is finish of you. And he pulled me into a office of a camp commander. But this camp commander was not there, anymore. So there were other officers there, SS officers. And he said to them, I brought a guy who is black marketing here in-- in the camp. But before he brought me in, I asked him, what do you want from me? I paying for these. Whatever I-- I did with you, any business, I pay you for. You know, but now, it's finished. Now, you are going-- going to the [NON-ENGLISH]. This means get a-- a trail. Judged.

speaker: interviewee
text: What's gonna-- what's gonna be with you? So those officers asked me-- asked him, what it this? He said, this guy is black marketing. So they asked me, what do you have? Say, I have, yeah, I have cigarettes. Where do you have those cigarettes? So I pull up my shirt. And the cigarettes, they fell down. I had 10 packs of cigarettes, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they asked me, where did get them? I said, I bought it by-- by Simonlener His name was Franz Simonlener. He was [NON-ENGLISH]. I bought them by him. So they asked-- they looked on him. He said, this is not true. Because he thought that he's going to get away with it very fast. So they went in in the other room, and they made some kind of a decision. And they came back, and they start to beat me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Three guys with-- with wooden sticks start to beat me all over my body. And when they beat me, they broke my nose, and they broke my eyes here, and they broke my head. Notice then that I got the blood into my mouth from nose. So I fell down. I knew that I have to fall down. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I fell down on the floor, and the blood from my mouth got out on-- on the floor. So one said to the other one, he's finished. He's-- let's call the order, and let him take it. You know, they going to put him back wherever they run the [INAUDIBLE] bodies.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure, I was lying on the floor. They called order. And they came with the-- with the-- how you call that-- shovel car. Whatever, [NON-ENGLISH] the hand car. They lugged me out on this. And they took me out from there. And they give the man order to bring me up into the Hujowa Gulka with the bodies were lying, and there was more. And they burn it down. On the way up there, I said to those two guys, listen. I'm alive. So they said, keep quiet. Be quiet. When we came up  to the-- the place there. So they told me, now you can get away. And I got away. I went to a friend of mine. And he helped me to wash up from the blood. And I was-- I was wearing a bandage. And a few days later, I was-- I was no more-- I was not going any more to appells, because I was killed. So I couldn't go to appells. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: I was hidden in a barrack. Then came-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Great.

speaker: interviewee
text: --then came the well-known Schindler List. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I didn't know nothing about nothing that my name was on the-- the list.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you name get on the list? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know nothing. After the war, I got to know. So when I was staying on the appellplatz with the rest of them, the Schindler List was called out name by name. And came to L, they called Victor Leserkiewicz. So I went out, and I stood with them all together. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Some people say that they pay money for it. I don't know. I didn't pay a penny, because I didn't have it then. I had a little blood on myself, but I didn't have no penny. And the same day, we were loaded into the cattle trucks, and came to Gross-Rosen. In Gross-Rosen was another split. The list of Schindler's was separated, and other people were separated again. [BACKGROUND NOISE] because the front of the Russians were closer to Krakow, they wanted to get people from-- from concentration camp Plaszow. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were in the Gross-Rosen for three or four days. I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me, but where is Gross-Rosen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gross-Rosen is in Germany. Now, it's in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was on the border between Poland-- yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was on the border in Germany. And there was [NON-ENGLISH]. It was the same thing. It was the same like it was in Plaszow, because they have the ovens there, and Plaszow didn't have the ovens. And in Gross-Rosen, we were separated. Schindlerjuden were separate, and the other Jews were separate. We were there for three days. Then we were loaded into, again, to a cattle car, and brought to Brinnlitz. In Brinnlitz, I said that I'm a electrician, what I was, not knowing nothing. But I was. So they gave me a job in electric [NON-ENGLISH]. That just means electric factory. And at then end, in the electric factory, we had a foreman who was German SS-- only one, no more [INAUDIBLE]. Only one. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he was to give work out. You with him, go to this job. You're with him. You're going to do this. And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I want to go so fast through Brinnlitz because in Brinnlitz, we had different things too. In Brinnlitz, there were a few little boys. The-- the commander of the Brinnlitz, his name was Liepold, who was officer of SS. He was the commander of the Brinnlitz Schindler factory. But [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] he didn't have nothing to say. Schindler didn't let him into the factory. He had only to stay outside the factory. But no Jew went out because everything was inside. The factory was inside, the living quarter was inside, so that the-- the-- the Gestapo had nothing to do with us. Only Schindler had. His orders were order.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said there was one SS man there.

speaker: interviewee
text: One, I said. He was only to the electric.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, just in one section.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because-- because they were bringing the big-- big machines in. Schindler moved the whole machines from Krakow to Brinnlitz So they were bringing-- Were these electric generators, or-- Everything, everything. So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the Gestapo had nothing to do with-- with the Jews in Schindler. And he didn't let them do nothing. They had to only to watch outside that nobody will get away from the-- from the camp. But inside, they had nothing to do. This SS man who was my boss was only one in the whole factory, because he was electrician. And they didn't have probably no electric, so he was telling people what to do and how to do it. They took somebody assessment. What happened to me then in-- in Brinnlitz, this foreman does assessment, give me a job to go to connect a welder. I knew that there-- there is three wires to connect-- hard wires. This I knew. I knew that I had to put one clip on one wire, and the other clip on the other wire, and the third clip on third wire. This I knew. But I didn't know how to do it. But I knew. The-- the order was done. I took a ladder. I went up. The-- the wires were going around the factory near-- near the ceiling. And I took one wire. I hook it up. I took the other wire. By the third wire, I touched the other wire, and I blew it up, the fuses in the whole shop, in the whole factory. And I dropped from the-- from the-- when I burned out, when the spark came up, I jumped off the ladder.

speaker: interviewer
text: My God.

speaker: interviewee
text: I jump off the ladder. I fell down. Of sure, there was no-- no electric. So he came run. He came running, this SS man. He came running to me, and he said, you don't know what  you're doing. So he went-- somebody went to our shop, and plugged all the-- the main switch. They took the wires off, and they put the switch back. The light was on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he send me back to the shop, and he told me, you go to the shop, and I'll be there pretty soon. Instead of shop, I went to Schindler. I didn't know Schindler. He didn't know me. I went to Schindler, and I said, Mrs. Schindler-- she was by the door. I said, Mrs. Schindler, I'm in trouble now. She said, what happened? So I told her what happened. So she called him. Oskar, somebody is here to see you. He came up to the door and asked me what happened. So I told him what happened. I did this and this. Oh, he said, oh yeah. We didn't have no light for a few minutes. All right. He said, you go to your shop, and I will come there.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to my shop, but knowing that something going to happen with me, not knowing what going to happen to me. I know that Schindler know about it. Then I know that nothing wrong going to happen to me-- maybe beating, maybe beating. Schindler came to the shop, so waiting until the SS man came. The SS man came. Schindler said to him, I need an electrician.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he say, all right. I give you electrician. He looked through. There was a group of electrician, 10 people. He looked through the whole group. Take this guy. Then he said, no, I want this guy, and he pointed on me. I want him. Oh, he's not an electrician. He doesn't know what the hell he's doing. He said, doesn't matter. I need-- I need this guy. You let me take him. He said, all right. Go ahead take him. Take him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And since this time I didn't go to no work at all to the end of the war. And this was my finish story in-- in Brinnlitz. There I was liberated. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I have to come back to something what I didn't tell you. There was a time in Brinnlitz where people who came from Budzyn, Majdanek, very tough guys, very tough guys. There were officers in Polish army, Jews from the Polish army. By the end of the war, they kicked them into the concentration camps, Russian boys. And they create a underground organization-- partisans. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And one of those people came to me-- this was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] before-- excuse me-- [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: --before I burn out the light in the-- Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: --In the [? shop. ?] And they ask me if I want to join the underground. So I said yes. Why not? The whole life I went through, I'm going to go again in another few-- few years. We didn't know how long going to take.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: So what happened, I knew nobody. I knew only five people in my unit. The five-- there was five units. I knew there was five units. But I-- I know only five people in my unit.

speaker: interviewer
text: How big were the units?

speaker: interviewee
text: Five people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, just five people in each unit?

speaker: interviewee
text: Five people in a unit. But the whole thing was five units to five people, 25 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, this is in the resistance group?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in the resistance group. So what-- what we did, we were breaking into magazines, to all kind of things, and bringing out, helping people with-- with food. And [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] the five people in the unit, you couldn't know the other five, because if the Gestapo caught you, so you could, if they press on you, you could give up only five. The other five you didn't know. You didn't know. You didn't know even the men who were-- was the-- the chief of the partisans.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had no idea that the Russians were getting closer and closer at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't have no idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had no more-- because you said--

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't have no idea then. No idea at all. After the war, when the war, when Schindler was getting away-- now, I want to tell you something. Schindler knew something about this partisan group. The partisan group [? back ?] got ammunition from outside, from the Czech-- Czech partisans. The connection was done, so they sent in into the camp ammunition, guns, and rifles. And we were running, the 25 people. I know about 25 now. But then when I'm talking about, I knew only five people. The all five people had guns on them-- every one from the five people. One was Joel Lipschitz. The other one was--
